deck: Mechanics of Materials & Machine Element Design

topics.title: Classes of engineering materials
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Metals (crystalline, ductile, stiff, conductive), polymers (molecular chains, low stiffness, temperature-sensitive), ceramics (ionic/covalent, hard, brittle, refractory), composites (matrix + reinforcement, anisotropic, high specific stiffness/strength). Selection trades stiffness, strength, density, toughness, temperature capability, and cost. Stiffness is structure-insensitive (bond stretching); strength is structure-sensitive (defect motion) — the key split behind what processing can and cannot fix.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk through the four classes of materials — what microstructure gives each its character, and what failure mode dominates each?
key_ideas: Metals: metallic bonding + dislocations → ductile, stiff, conductive
Polymers: chain mobility sets behavior — temperature- and rate-sensitive
Ceramics: strong ionic/covalent bonds, no dislocation slip → hard but brittle (flaw-dominated)
Composites: matrix + reinforcement, anisotropic, best stiffness/strength per weight
Stiffness = bond stretching (structure-insensitive); strength = dislocation resistance (structure-sensitive)
Selection = stiffness, strength, density, toughness, max temperature, cost

cards.question: Why are ceramics strong in compression but unreliable in tension?
cards.answer: No dislocation slip means no plasticity to blunt cracks — tensile strength is governed by the worst flaw (Griffith), so it's statistical and far below compressive strength.

cards.question: All steels share E ≈ 200 GPa, yet their yield strengths span nearly a factor of 10. Why — and what does that mean when a part is too flexible vs too weak?
cards.answer: Stiffness is structure-insensitive: it comes from stretching metallic bonds, which alloying and heat treatment barely touch. Strength is structure-sensitive: it is resistance to dislocation motion, tunable via grain size, solutes, precipitates, martensite. So heat treatment can rescue a weak part, but a too-flexible one needs new geometry (more I) or a different material class — no steel is stiffer than another.

cards.question: Why is a composite laminate's strength quoted with a layup and load direction while steel gets one number — and which failure modes have no metal analogue?
cards.answer: Fibers carry the load: along-fiber properties are excellent but matrix-dominated directions are roughly an order of magnitude weaker, so properties depend on ply stack and load angle. Steel's random polycrystal averages out to isotropy. Composite design means aligning plies with load paths and checking modes metals don't have: transverse ply cracking, delamination between plies, and bearing failure at bolted joints.

topics.title: Crystal structures: FCC, BCC, HCP
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: FCC (Al, Cu, Ni, austenite): 12 slip systems, ductile even cold, no ductile-brittle transition. BCC (ferrite, Cr, W): slip is thermally activated → ductile-brittle transition temperature, higher yield, strain-rate sensitive. HCP (Mg, Ti, Zn): few slip systems → limited ductility, twinning, strong texture effects. Packing and slip systems drive mechanical behavior; structure also sets density and influences thermal/electrical/magnetic properties.
topics.recall_prompt: Compare FCC, BCC and HCP: slip systems, ductility, and which common metals/steel phases belong to each.
key_ideas: FCC: close-packed, 12 slip systems → ductile at all temperatures (Al, Cu, austenite)
BCC: thermally-activated slip → ductile-brittle transition (ferrite, W)
HCP: few slip systems → low ductility, twinning, anisotropy (Mg, Ti)
Slip systems = close-packed planes × directions; ~5 independent systems needed for polycrystal ductility
Crystal structure also sets density, conductivity, magnetic behavior

cards.question: Why does steel (BCC ferrite) have a ductile-brittle transition temperature but aluminum (FCC) doesn't?
cards.answer: BCC slip is thermally activated — at low temperature dislocations can't move fast enough, so cleavage beats slip. FCC close-packed slip needs no thermal help, so it stays ductile to cryogenic temperatures. (Liberty ship failures.)

cards.question: Which phase of steel is FCC, and what does that explain about it?
cards.answer: Austenite (γ). FCC explains its high ductility/formability at temperature and its higher carbon solubility than BCC ferrite — the basis of heat-treating.

cards.question: Why is magnesium sheet formed warm (~250 °C) when aluminum forms fine at room temperature?
cards.answer: A polycrystal needs about five independent slip systems to deform into an arbitrary shape (von Mises criterion). FCC aluminum has them at any temperature. HCP magnesium at room temperature slips essentially only on basal planes — too few independent systems — so grains can't accommodate their neighbors and the sheet twins or cracks. Heating activates prismatic/pyramidal slip and formability appears.

topics.title: Hall-Petch & grain size strengthening
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k/\sqrt{d}$ — finer grains raise yield strength. Mechanism: grain boundaries block dislocation motion; dislocations pile up at boundaries until stress concentrations activate slip in the neighbor. More boundary area = more obstacles. Breaks down at nanocrystalline sizes (~<20 nm) where boundary sliding takes over (inverse Hall-Petch). Grain size is controlled by solidification rate, cold work + recrystallization, and heat treatment (grain growth at temperature).
topics.recall_prompt: Explain how grain size controls yield strength — the equation, the physical mechanism, how you control it, and where the law breaks down.
key_ideas: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k/\sqrt{d}$ — finer grains → higher yield
Mechanism: boundaries block dislocations → pile-ups must trigger slip across
Control: solidification rate, cold work + recrystallization, avoid grain growth at temperature
Inverse Hall-Petch below ~20 nm: grain-boundary sliding takes over
Strengthening without ductility loss — unlike most strengthening mechanisms

cards.question: Hall-Petch is unusual among strengthening mechanisms — what trade-off does it largely avoid, and why?
cards.answer: It strengthens without the usual ductility penalty: boundaries impede dislocations but grains still deform compatibly, unlike solute/precipitate strengthening which embrittles more. Fine grain also improves toughness.

cards.question: Why is the heat-affected zone next to a weld often the weakest, least tough region even though its composition is unchanged?
cards.answer: The near-fusion HAZ gets hot enough for grain growth: d rises, so $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k/\sqrt{d}$ falls, and coarse grains also hurt toughness (longer pile-ups, easier cleavage). Any cold-work strengthening recrystallizes away too. That's why welded designs take allowables from the HAZ, not the parent plate — and why fine-carbide, grain-growth-resistant steels exist.

cards.question: Nanocrystalline metals were supposed to be ultra-strong — why does Hall-Petch stop working below ~20 nm grains?
cards.answer: The mechanism needs dislocation pile-ups at boundaries, and a grain only a few dislocation spacings wide can't host one — the source-and-pile-up picture dies. Deformation switches to grain-boundary sliding and diffusion, which get EASIER with more boundary area: strength plateaus, then falls (inverse Hall-Petch).

topics.title: Yield point phenomena & Luder's bands
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Low-carbon steel shows an upper yield point (stress to tear dislocations from carbon/nitrogen atmospheres — Cottrell atmospheres), a drop to a lower yield point, then a plateau while Luder's bands (fronts of localized yielding) sweep the gauge length. After the plateau, normal strain hardening resumes. Matters in forming: Luder's bands leave visible 'stretcher strains' on sheet surfaces — suppressed by temper rolling or by using interstitial-free steel.
topics.recall_prompt: Sketch the low-carbon steel stress-strain curve through yield: what causes the upper/lower yield points and the plateau, and why does it matter in sheet forming?
key_ideas: Upper yield: stress to unpin dislocations from C/N solute atmospheres (Cottrell)
Lower yield + plateau: Luder's bands sweep the gauge at roughly constant stress
After the band front passes everything has yielded → normal strain hardening resumes
Forming defect: stretcher strains on sheet; fixed by temper rolling or IF steel
Strain aging: atmospheres re-form over time → yield point returns

cards.question: Why does the yield point of low-carbon steel come back after the part sits for months (strain aging)?
cards.answer: Carbon/nitrogen atoms diffuse back to dislocations and re-form Cottrell atmospheres, re-pinning them — so the upper yield point and Luder's behavior reappear.

cards.question: Sketch the engineering stress–strain curve of annealed low-carbon steel from zero through fracture, with the yield region drawn honestly.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: engineering stress vs engineering strain
- Linear elastic rise to a sharp peak — the upper yield point
- Abrupt drop to the lower yield point
- Flat, slightly ragged plateau at the lower yield stress: yield-point elongation of ~1–3% strain (Luder's bands sweeping the gauge)
- Smooth rising strain-hardening curve after the plateau, peaking at the UTS
- Falling engineering stress beyond UTS (necking), ending at fracture ×
- Valid only for annealed/aged low-C steel — pre-strained or temper-rolled material rejoins the curve smoothly with no peak or plateau

cards.question: How does a ~1% temper-rolling skin pass eliminate stretcher strains — and why does the fix expire on the shelf?
cards.answer: The skin pass pre-strains the sheet just past the yield-point elongation, so the Luder's fronts have already swept through; later forming starts on the smooth strain-hardening curve and leaves no bands. But strain aging lets C/N atmospheres re-pin the dislocations over weeks to months (faster warm), restoring the yield point — so sheet is formed within a window after temper rolling, or you buy interstitial-free steel.

topics.title: Phases of steel & TTT/CCT diagrams
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Austenite (γ, FCC, stable hot, dissolves C) transforms on cooling: slow → ferrite (α, BCC, soft) + pearlite (lamellar ferrite + cementite); moderate → bainite; quench → martensite (diffusionless shear into body-centered tetragonal, supersaturated C, hard and brittle — needs tempering). TTT diagrams map isothermal transformation (the 'C-curve' with the nose to avoid for martensite); CCT shifts the curves right/down for continuous cooling — the realistic case. Hardenability = how easy it is to miss the nose.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk through what happens to austenite at different cooling rates — name each phase, its structure and properties, and how TTT vs CCT diagrams encode this.
key_ideas: Austenite (FCC) is the hot parent phase; carbon dissolves in it
Slow cool → ferrite + pearlite (soft, ductile); moderate → bainite
Quench past the nose → martensite: diffusionless, BCT, hard/brittle, must temper
TTT = isothermal C-curves; CCT = continuous cooling (curves shift right/down)
Hardenability: alloying pushes the nose right, letting thick sections harden

cards.question: Why must you cool past the 'nose' of the TTT curve to form martensite, and what do alloying elements do to that requirement?
cards.answer: The nose is where diffusional transformation (pearlite/bainite) is fastest; beat it and diffusion never gets a chance, so austenite shears to martensite below Ms. Alloying (Cr, Mo, Ni) slows diffusion, pushing the nose right — slower quenches still harden (better hardenability, less distortion).

cards.question: Why is as-quenched martensite brittle, and what does tempering actually do?
cards.answer: Carbon trapped in the BCT lattice creates huge internal strain with no slip capacity. Tempering lets carbon precipitate as fine carbides — strain relaxes, toughness returns, at modest hardness cost.

cards.question: Sketch the TTT diagram for a eutectoid steel and overlay two cooling paths: one ending in coarse pearlite, one in martensite.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: temperature (vertical) vs log time (horizontal)
- Horizontal line near 727 °C — above it austenite is stable
- C-shaped start and finish curves with the nose around 550 °C at roughly 1 s
- Products labeled: coarse pearlite near the top, fine pearlite toward the nose, bainite below the nose
- Horizontal Ms (≈220 °C for eutectoid) and Mf lines at the bottom — martensite fraction depends on temperature reached, not time
- Path 1: gentle cooling curve crossing start and finish lines high up → coarse pearlite
- Path 2: steep quench missing the nose entirely, continuing down past Ms → martensite
- Caveat noted: strictly isothermal data — a real continuous quench needs the CCT, with curves shifted right and down

cards.question: TTT vs CCT — which matches a real quench, and in which direction does reading the wrong one bite?
cards.answer: TTT is isothermal: instant-quench to one temperature, hold, watch — it matches salt-bath practice like austempering. A real quench cools continuously; its map is the CCT, whose curves sit at longer times and lower temperatures. Reading a continuous quench against TTT overestimates how easily pearlite forms — you can predict 'soft' where the part actually hardens to martensite (how weld HAZs crack unexpectedly) — and the critical cooling rate it implies is faster than the part really needs.

topics.title: Heat treatment of metals
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Annealing: heat + slow cool → soft, ductile, stress-relieved (recovery → recrystallization → grain growth). Normalizing: air cool → finer pearlite, more uniform. Quench + temper: martensite then controlled toughness recovery. Case hardening (carburizing, nitriding): hard surface, tough core. Precipitation (age) hardening for Al/Ni alloys: solution treat, quench, age to grow coherent precipitates that pin dislocations — peak-aged vs over-aged.
topics.recall_prompt: For anneal, normalize, quench+temper, case hardening, and age hardening: what's the thermal cycle, what microstructure results, and when do you pick each?
key_ideas: Anneal = slow cool → softest, most ductile (recovery → recrystallization → grain growth)
Normalize = air cool → finer, more uniform pearlite than anneal
Quench + temper = martensite, then carbide precipitation for toughness
Case hardening: hard wear surface + tough core (gears, shafts)
Age hardening (Al, Ni): coherent precipitates pin dislocations; over-aging coarsens them and softens

cards.question: Why does over-aging soften an age-hardened aluminum alloy?
cards.answer: Precipitates coarsen: fewer, larger, less coherent particles are easier for dislocations to bypass (Orowan looping) than the fine coherent ones they must cut at peak age — strength drops.

cards.question: For a gear that must survive both flank pitting and root bending fatigue, why does carburizing usually beat through-hardening?
cards.answer: Carburizing puts a hard, high-carbon martensite case exactly where contact stress and crack initiation live, over a tough low-carbon core that arrests cracks and takes shock — and the case transforms last, leaving residual surface compression that directly buys fatigue life. A through-hardened gear at case hardness would be brittle to the core; at core toughness it would pit. Case + core decouples the two requirements.

cards.question: Same steel, same austenitizing — one part furnace-cools, one air-cools. Which is stronger, and what lever did the cooling rate actually pull?
cards.answer: The air-cooled (normalized) part: faster cooling undercools the austenite more, so pearlite nucleates at lower temperature with finer lamellar spacing and smaller grains — more interface area blocking dislocations. The furnace-cooled anneal maximizes softness, ductility and machinability instead. Same chemistry; the only knob was transformation temperature.

topics.title: Hardness testing & strength correlations
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Hardness is resistance to localized plastic indentation — a constrained compression test. Because the surrounding elastic material confines the plastic zone, mean indentation pressure ≈ 3× the flow stress (Tabor), which is why hardness tracks strength: for steels $S_{ut} \approx 3.45\,HB$ in MPa (≈ 500·HB in psi). Brinell: 10 mm ball, large indent that averages coarse microstructure — castings, weldments; unreliable above ~650 HB. Rockwell: depth-based, direct-reading, fast — shop QC; each scale (B, C, …) is its own number. Vickers: 136° diamond pyramid, geometrically similar so one load-independent scale; microhardness traverses map case depth. Hardness says nothing about toughness or ductility — a file-hard part can be glass-brittle.
topics.recall_prompt: Compare Brinell, Rockwell and Vickers — what each measures and when you'd pick each — then explain physically why hardness correlates with tensile strength and what it cannot tell you.
key_ideas: Hardness = resistance to plastic indentation; elastic constraint makes indentation pressure ≈ 3× flow stress (Tabor)
Steel correlation: $S_{ut} \approx 3.45\,HB$ (MPa) — a fast, near-nondestructive strength estimate and heat-treat QC check
Brinell: big ball indent averages coarse microstructure (castings, welds); don't trust above ~650 HB
Rockwell: reads indentation depth directly — fast shop test, but scales (B, C) don't interconvert simply
Vickers: geometrically similar pyramid → one continuous load-independent scale; microhardness maps case-depth profiles
Hardness ranks strength, not toughness or ductility — it cannot see brittleness or quench cracks

cards.question: Why does $S_{ut} \approx 3.45\,HB$ (MPa) work for steels — what is hardness physically measuring?
cards.answer: The indenter drives a plastic zone into elastically confined material; that constraint roughly triples the pressure needed versus uniaxial flow, so hardness ≈ 3× the flow stress at a representative strain of ~8% (Tabor). For steels, flow stress at that strain tracks $S_{ut}$, giving the 3.45·HB rule. The constant drifts across material classes and work-hardening states — don't carry it to aluminum or cold-worked parts.

cards.question: Rockwell C on the shop floor, Vickers in the lab — why does each own its niche, and when does Rockwell flatly mislead?
cards.answer: Rockwell reads depth directly: seconds per test, no optics — ideal QC, but each scale has a valid range and the indent is relatively deep. Vickers' geometrically similar pyramid gives one continuous number at any load, with indents tiny enough to traverse a carburized case in cross-section. Rockwell misleads on thin cases, coatings, or thin stock: the indenter's plastic zone punches through and reads the soft substrate underneath.

cards.question: Two parts both read 45 HRC — one quench-and-tempered alloy steel, one untempered martensite of a leaner steel. Hardness says they're equal. What does it miss?
cards.answer: Everything crack-related: the Q&T part is tough, while untempered martensite can have a small fraction of its impact energy plus locked-in quench stresses and microcracks. Hardness measures resistance to plastic flow only — it ranks strength but is blind to toughness, ductility, and residual stress, so accept parts on hardness plus evidence of temper (or an impact requirement), never hardness alone.

topics.title: Polymers: amorphous vs semi-crystalline
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Amorphous (PC, PMMA, ABS): random chains, glass transition Tg governs — clear, dimensionally stable, soften gradually; below Tg glassy/brittle-ish, above rubbery. Semi-crystalline (PE, PP, Nylon, PEEK): ordered lamellae in amorphous matrix, sharp melting point Tm, opaque, tougher, better chemical/fatigue resistance, more shrinkage/warpage in molding. Properties come from chain structure: molecular weight, branching, cross-linking (thermosets don't melt), side-group bulk.
topics.recall_prompt: Compare amorphous vs semi-crystalline polymers: structure, thermal transitions (Tg vs Tm), and what each means for molding and end-use.
key_ideas: Amorphous: random chains, Tg-governed, transparent, gradual softening, low mold shrinkage
Semi-crystalline: lamellae + amorphous, sharp Tm, opaque, tough, chemically resistant
Semi-crystalline shrinks/warps more in molding (crystallization is densification)
Cross-linking → thermoset: no melting, decomposes instead
Chain mobility is everything: temperature and strain-rate sensitivity follow from it

cards.question: Why do semi-crystalline plastics shrink and warp more than amorphous ones in injection molding?
cards.answer: Crystallization is a packing/densification event — crystals are denser than melt, and crystallinity varies with local cooling rate, so shrinkage is bigger and non-uniform → warpage. Amorphous resins just freeze in place.

cards.question: Polypropylene's Tg is around 0 °C yet PP parts hold shape at 100 °C; polycarbonate's Tg is ~147 °C and it's structurally useless above it. Reconcile.
cards.answer: Tg is the transition of the amorphous fraction only. In semi-crystalline PP the crystallites stay solid up to Tm (~165 °C) and act as physical crosslinks, so between Tg and Tm you get a tough, leathery solid — that IS its service window, and the rubbery amorphous phase is why it's tough. Amorphous PC is all glass: above Tg the whole material goes rubbery. Rule: max use temperature tracks Tg for amorphous polymers but approaches (a margin below) Tm for semi-crystalline ones.

cards.question: A plastic bracket is ductile in a slow pull test but shatters in a drop test at the same temperature. Why, and what's the design takeaway?
cards.answer: Yielding needs chain segments to move within the load's timescale, and raising strain rate is equivalent to lowering temperature (time–temperature superposition). In a millisecond impact the chains can't relax, the material behaves as if below Tg, and it fails by brittle crazing/cracking. Takeaway: qualify polymers at service strain rate AND temperature — a quasi-static datasheet curve says nothing about impact.

topics.title: Elementary stress formulas (the big six)
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Axial: $\delta = FL/EA$, $\sigma = F/A$. Bending: $\sigma = My/I$. Torsion: $\tau = Tr/J$, twist $\phi = TL/JG$. Transverse shear: $\tau = VQ/It$. Thin-wall pressure vessel: hoop $\sigma_h = Pr/t$, axial $\sigma_a = Pr/2t$. Each assumes linear elasticity, small deflections, and its own geometry caveats (e.g., $Tr/J$ only for circular sections; $Pr/t$ only for $r/t \gtrsim 10$).
topics.recall_prompt: Write the six elementary stress/deflection formulas (axial, bending, torsion + twist, transverse shear, hoop) and state the key assumption that limits each.
key_ideas: $\delta = FL/EA$ — axial; uniform section, centric load
$\sigma = My/I$ — bending; plane sections remain plane, linear elastic
$\tau = Tr/J$, $\phi = TL/JG$ — torsion; circular sections only (else warping)
$\tau = VQ/It$ — transverse shear; max at neutral axis, $Q$ = first moment above the cut
$\sigma_h = Pr/t$, $\sigma_a = Pr/2t$ — thin wall ($r/t \gtrsim 10$); hoop is double axial
All assume linear elasticity + small deflection; superpose for combined loading

cards.question: Why does $\tau = Tr/J$ fail for non-circular shafts, and what happens physically?
cards.answer: Non-circular cross-sections warp out of plane under torsion — plane sections don't remain plane, so the linear shear distribution assumption collapses. Max shear moves to the midpoint of the long side (rectangle), and you need torsion constants, not $J$.

cards.question: A cylindrical pressure vessel fails — along which direction does the crack run, and why?
cards.answer: Longitudinally (axially): hoop stress $Pr/t$ is twice the axial $Pr/2t$, and hoop stress opens an axial crack. Sausages split lengthwise.

cards.question: Two beams with equal cross-sectional area: solid square vs I-beam. Why does the I-beam carry several times the moment — and which loading makes it exactly the wrong choice?
cards.answer: Bending stress is $\sigma = My/I$, and parking area far from the neutral axis grows I (≈ A·d²) without adding material — the flanges work at full stress, the web just connects them. The same trick fails in torsion: an I-section is an OPEN thin-wall shape with torsional stiffness ∝ Σbt³/3, nearly worthless — it twists and warps. For torsion use a closed tube or box, where shear flow circulates around enclosed area.

cards.question: Why does transverse shear govern short stubby beams, wood, and laminates — but almost never long steel beams?
cards.answer: Peak bending stress grows with span (M ∝ PL) while peak shear $\tau = 1.5V/A$ (rectangle, at the neutral axis) doesn't — their ratio scales with L/h. Steel shears at ~0.58$\sigma_y$, so bending wins except at absurd stubbiness. Wood and composite laminates carry weak planes (grain, interlaminar) at ~a tenth of bending strength, lying exactly where $VQ/It$ peaks — short spans split or delaminate at the neutral axis first.

topics.title: Mohr's circle & stress transformation
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Plane stress state $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$ maps to a circle: center $(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2$, radius $R = \sqrt{((\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$. Principal stresses = center ± R (where shear is zero); max in-plane shear = R at 45° from principal. Rotations double on the circle (2θ). Don't forget the third principal stress (0 in plane stress) — max absolute shear may be out of plane.
topics.recall_prompt: Construct Mohr's circle from (σx, σy, τxy): center, radius, principal stresses, max shear, and the 45°/2θ relationships — plus the out-of-plane trap.
key_ideas: Center $= (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2$, radius $R = \sqrt{((\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
Principals $\sigma_{1,2}$ = center ± R; shear is zero on principal planes
Max in-plane shear = R, on planes 45° from principal
Angles double on the circle: physical θ → 2θ
Plane stress still has $\sigma_3 = 0$: max absolute shear can be $\sigma_1/2$ out of plane

cards.question: When is the true maximum shear stress NOT the in-plane Mohr's circle radius, and what is it instead?
cards.answer: When $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ have the same sign (both tensile, say): then $\tau_{max} = |\sigma_1 - \sigma_3|/2 = \sigma_1/2$ with $\sigma_3 = 0$ out of plane — bigger than the in-plane radius. Tresca checks must use all three circles.

cards.question: Sketch Mohr's circle for σx = 80 MPa, σy = 20 MPa, τxy = 40 MPa, marking everything a grader should find.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: σ horizontal, τ vertical, equal scales
- Plotted points X(80, 40) and Y(20, −40) — opposite shear signs per your convention — joined by a diameter
- Center on the σ-axis at ((80+20)/2, 0) = (50, 0)
- Radius R = √(30² + 40²) = 50
- Principal stresses where the circle crosses the σ-axis: σ1 = 100 MPa, σ2 = 0 (the circle passes through the origin)
- Max in-plane shear = R = 50 MPa at the top/bottom, where σ = 50 MPa
- 2θp = arctan(40/30) ≈ 53° on the circle → θp ≈ 27° in the part
- Note: σ3 = 0 coincides with σ2 here, so max absolute shear is still 50 MPa

cards.question: Twist a piece of chalk until it breaks. Why does the fracture spiral at 45° — and what does the matching steel shaft failure look like?
cards.answer: Pure shear plots as a Mohr's circle centered at the origin: principals +τ and −τ on planes 45° to the axis. Chalk is brittle — governed by max normal stress — so the crack follows the peak-tension plane: a 45° helix. A ductile shaft fails on the max-shear plane instead and breaks off square. The fracture surface tells you which failure mode governed.

topics.title: Shear & moment diagrams, shear flow, shear center
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: $dV/dx = -w(x)$, $dM/dx = V(x)$: moment is the integral of shear; moment extrema where V crosses zero; concentrated forces step V, concentrated moments step M. Shear flow $q = VQ/I$ sizes welds/rivets/glue joining built-up sections. The shear center is the point a transverse load must pass through to avoid twisting — off the centroid for open thin-wall sections (channels): load through the centroid of a channel twists it.
topics.recall_prompt: State the V-M differential relationships and use them to sketch diagrams; then explain shear flow and why a channel section twists unless loaded through its shear center.
key_ideas: $dM/dx = V$, $dV/dx = -w$: slopes; M extremum where V = 0
Point load steps V; point moment steps M
Shear flow $q = VQ/I$ [force/length] sizes fasteners/welds in built-up beams
Shear center: load line that produces bending without twist
Open sections (channel): shear center lies outside the web, off the centroid

cards.question: Why does a channel section twist when you load it through its centroid?
cards.answer: The internal shear flow in the flanges forms a couple; its resultant acts at the shear center (offset from the centroid, outside the web). A centroidal load is then eccentric to that resultant → net torque → twist.

cards.question: Sketch the shear and moment diagrams, aligned one above the other, for a simply supported beam of span L with a point load P at midspan.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Reactions P/2 at each support
- V diagram: constant at +P/2 over the left half, step down by P at midspan, constant at −P/2 to the right support, closing to zero
- M diagram: straight line from 0 up to PL/4 at midspan, straight back down to 0 — a triangle
- Slope check labeled: dM/dx = V, so the moment slopes are ±P/2
- Peak moment exactly where V crosses zero (midspan)
- No distributed load anywhere → V piecewise constant, M piecewise linear

cards.question: A built-up wood beam is nailed together from planks. How does shear flow set the nail spacing, and where must the nails crowd?
cards.answer: The interface must carry longitudinal shear flow $q = VQ/I$ (force per length) or the planks slide and the section stops acting as one I. Each nail of capacity F permits spacing $s = F/q$. Since q follows V, spacing tightens where shear is largest — at the supports for distributed loads — and can relax where V is small. Get it wrong and the splice shears apart at the support, collapsing the composite stiffness.

topics.title: Column buckling
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Euler: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/(KL)^2$ — elastic instability, governed by stiffness $E$ and geometry (I, length), not strength. K encodes end conditions: pinned-pinned 1, fixed-free 2, fixed-pinned 0.7, fixed-fixed 0.5. Valid for slender columns (high $KL/r$); intermediate columns fail by inelastic buckling (Johnson parabola); short columns just yield. Buckling occurs about the axis of minimum I.
topics.recall_prompt: Derive/state the Euler buckling load: where each term comes from, the K values for the four end conditions, and when Euler stops applying.
key_ideas: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/(KL)^2$ — instability load, scales with E·I, not yield strength
K: pinned-pinned 1, fixed-free 2, fixed-pinned ~0.7, fixed-fixed 0.5
Buckles about the weakest axis (minimum I)
Slenderness $KL/r$ decides: Euler (slender) vs Johnson (intermediate) vs yield (short)
Doubling unsupported length cuts capacity 4×

cards.question: Why doesn't making a column out of stronger steel raise its Euler buckling load?
cards.answer: Buckling is an elastic *stiffness* instability: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/(KL)^2$ contains E and I, not $\sigma_y$. All steels share E ≈ 200 GPa — you need more I (geometry) or shorter effective length, not strength.

cards.question: Euler's formula dangerously overpredicts the strength of intermediate columns — why exactly, and what replaces it?
cards.answer: Euler assumes the column is still elastic at buckling. For stockier columns the predicted critical stress exceeds the proportional limit, the tangent modulus drops below E, and real capacity falls under Euler's curve. Practice uses the Johnson parabola: $S_y$ at zero slenderness, meeting Euler tangentially at $\sigma_{cr} = S_y/2$ — switch at the transition slenderness $(KL/r)_1 = \sqrt{2\pi^2E/S_y}$, Johnson below it, Euler above.

cards.question: You weld the base of a pinned-pinned column so it becomes fixed-pinned. How much buckling capacity did you buy — and why do codes not give you all of it?
cards.answer: K drops from 1.0 to a theoretical 0.7, and $P_{cr} \propto 1/K^2$ → about 2× capacity (fixed-fixed would be 4×). But real 'fixed' ends rotate — base plates flex, footings rock — giving back part of the gain, so design codes use degraded values (e.g., 0.8 instead of 0.7, 0.65 instead of 0.5). Claim theoretical K only when the foundation truly is rigid.

topics.title: Ductile failure theories: Von Mises, Tresca, Coulomb-Mohr
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Von Mises (distortion energy): yield when $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]} = \sigma_y$ — best fit to ductile test data. Tresca (max shear): $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1-\sigma_3)/2 = \sigma_y/2$ — simpler, conservative (inscribed hexagon). They agree in uniaxial tension, disagree most in pure shear (~15%, Tresca predicts failure earlier: $\tau_y = 0.5\sigma_y$ vs $0.577\sigma_y$). Ductile Coulomb-Mohr for materials with unequal tensile/compressive strengths (cast iron-ish ductile cases).
topics.recall_prompt: Compare Von Mises and Tresca: equations, geometric picture (ellipse vs hexagon), where they agree/disagree most, which is conservative, and when Coulomb-Mohr replaces both.
key_ideas: Von Mises = distortion energy: $\sigma_{VM}$ vs $\sigma_y$; ellipse; best fit to data
Tresca = max shear: $(\sigma_1-\sigma_3)/2$ vs $\sigma_y/2$; hexagon inscribed in the ellipse → conservative
Agree in uniaxial; max disagreement in pure shear: $\tau_y = 0.577\sigma_y$ (VM) vs $0.5\sigma_y$ (Tresca), ~15%
Use all three principal stresses — the out-of-plane zero matters
Coulomb-Mohr when $S_{ut} \neq S_{uc}$ (strength differs in tension vs compression)

cards.question: A shaft is in pure torsion. Which yields first by prediction — Von Mises or Tresca — and by how much do the predictions differ?
cards.answer: Tresca predicts yield earlier: $\tau_y = 0.5\,\sigma_y$ vs Von Mises $\tau_y = \sigma_y/\sqrt{3} = 0.577\,\sigma_y$ — about 15% apart, the maximum disagreement between the theories. Experiments side with Von Mises; Tresca is the conservative design choice.

cards.question: Why is hydrostatic pressure invisible to both Von Mises and Tresca?
cards.answer: Both depend only on principal stress *differences* (shear/distortion). Hydrostatic stress changes volume, not shape — and ductile yield is a shear (dislocation slip) phenomenon. A submarine hull doesn't yield from depth pressure alone.

cards.question: When must Coulomb-Mohr replace Von Mises/Tresca, and what extra material data does it need?
cards.answer: When tensile and compressive strengths differ — gray cast iron's $S_{uc}$ is 3–4× its $S_{ut}$. It needs both strengths, building the failure envelope tangent to the tension and compression Mohr circles: $\sigma_1/S_{ut} - \sigma_3/S_{uc} = 1/n$ in the mixed quadrant. A symmetric theory errs both ways: it wastes the real compressive capacity, and in torsion (σ1 = −σ3) it misses that failure is dominated by the weak tensile leg.

topics.title: Fracture mechanics: Griffith & stress intensity
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Sharp cracks concentrate stress; strength of brittle/cracked parts is governed by crack size, not material strength. $K_I = \beta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (Mode I opening; β = geometry factor); fracture when $K_I \ge K_{IC}$ (plane-strain fracture toughness, a material property). Griffith: crack grows when released elastic energy ≥ surface energy. Modes I/II/III = opening/sliding/tearing. The plastic zone at the tip ($r_p \sim (K/\sigma_y)^2$) must be small vs. crack/thickness for LEFM validity; thick sections → plane strain → lowest toughness.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain LEFM: K_I = βσ√(πa), what K_IC means, the three crack modes, the plastic-zone caveat, and the design triangle of stress–crack size–toughness.
key_ideas: $K_I = \beta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$; fracture when $K_I \ge K_{IC}$
$K_{IC}$ = plane-strain fracture toughness — a material property (thick = worst case)
Griffith energy balance: released strain energy vs new surface energy
Modes: I opening, II in-plane shear, III tearing — I dominates design
Plastic zone $\sim (K/\sigma_y)^2$ must stay small for LEFM to hold
Design triangle: allowable stress, detectable crack size, toughness — pick two

cards.question: Why does doubling the design stress quarter the allowable crack size?
cards.answer: $K_I = \beta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ at fixed $K_{IC}$: $a \propto 1/\sigma^2$. This is why inspection intervals and NDT detectability drive allowable stress in fracture-critical parts.

cards.question: Why is fracture toughness lowest in thick sections?
cards.answer: Thickness enforces plane strain: through-thickness contraction is constrained, triaxial tension builds at the crack tip, the plastic zone shrinks, and less energy dissipates before fracture — hence $K_{IC}$ (plane strain) is the conservative, quoted value.

cards.question: $K_I$ and $K_{IC}$ share the same units (MPa·√m) — what's the actual distinction, and what's the static-design analogy?
cards.answer: $K_I$ is the applied crack-driving force: it lives on the load side, computed from stress, crack size and geometry ($\beta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$), and changes with every load case. $K_{IC}$ is the material's measured resistance — a property like yield strength, varying only with material, temperature, thickness. The check $K_I < K_{IC}/n$ is exactly parallel to $\sigma < S_y/n$; confusing the two is like quoting 'the stress of 4340 steel'.

topics.title: Fatigue: endurance limits & mean-stress theories
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Cyclic stress below yield kills parts: crack initiates at a surface feature, propagates per cycle, then fast-fractures. S-N curve; steels show an endurance limit $S_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ (capped ~700 MPa), aluminum doesn't (use $S_f$ at $5\times10^8$). Marin factors knock $S_e'$ down for surface, size, load type, temperature, reliability. Mean stress matters: on the $\sigma_a$–$\sigma_m$ plot, Goodman (line to $S_{ut}$, standard), Soderberg (line to $S_y$, most conservative), Gerber (parabola, best data fit). Fatigue is driven by amplitude; mean tension reduces allowable amplitude.
topics.recall_prompt: Lay out the fatigue design framework: S-N and endurance limit, Marin knockdowns, then draw the Goodman/Soderberg/Gerber lines and explain when each is the right call.
key_ideas: Stages: initiation at surface → per-cycle growth (striations) → fast fracture
Steel endurance limit $S_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ (cap ~700 MPa); aluminum has none
Marin factors: surface, size, load, temperature, reliability multiply $S_e'$ down
Goodman: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ — the standard line
Soderberg uses $S_y$ (most conservative); Gerber parabola fits data best
Compressive mean stress helps — basis of shot peening

cards.question: Goodman vs Soderberg vs Gerber — same amplitude and mean stress: rank the safety factors they give, and say why.
cards.answer: Soderberg is most conservative (intercepts at yield $S_y$, also guards against first-cycle yield), Goodman next (intercepts $S_{ut}$), Gerber least conservative (parabola hugging the data). Use Goodman by default, Soderberg when any yielding is unacceptable.

cards.question: Why does shot peening extend fatigue life?
cards.answer: It plastically dimples the surface, leaving residual *compressive* stress where fatigue cracks initiate — the local mean stress drops, so the allowable amplitude rises and initiation is delayed. It also work-hardens the surface.

cards.question: Sketch the S-N curves for steel and aluminum on the same axes.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: stress amplitude S (vertical) vs cycles to failure N on a log scale, ~10³ to 10⁸⁺
- Both curves sloping down from the low-cycle end (around 0.9·Sut at 10³)
- Steel: a distinct knee at ~10⁶–10⁷ cycles, then a horizontal plateau — the endurance limit Se ≈ 0.5·Sut (capped ~700 MPa)
- Aluminum: no knee — the curve keeps falling, so only a fatigue strength Sf at a stated life (conventionally 5×10⁸) exists
- Labels: below Se steel lives indefinitely; aluminum never has a safe stress, only a design life
- Caveat: curves are for polished R = −1 lab specimens — Marin factors slide the whole curve down for real parts

cards.question: Sketch a Goodman diagram (σa vs σm) for a steel part, including the Gerber, Soderberg and first-cycle-yield lines and a proportional load line.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: alternating stress σa (vertical) vs mean stress σm (horizontal)
- Goodman: straight line from Se on the σa-axis to Sut on the σm-axis
- Soderberg: straight line from Se to Sy, everywhere inside Goodman
- Gerber: parabola from Se to Sut, bowing above Goodman
- Langer first-cycle-yield line σa + σm = Sy cutting the corner near the σm-axis
- Safe region = under the chosen fatigue line AND under the yield line
- Load line from the origin through the operating point, slope r = σa/σm; safety factor read as the distance ratio to the criterion along that line
- Compressive-mean side: data flattens (σa ≈ Se holds) — don't extend Goodman's penalty below σm = 0

topics.title: Fatigue crack growth: Paris law & damage tolerance
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Between initiation and fast fracture, a crack grows a measurable amount per cycle: $da/dN = C\,\Delta K^m$ (Paris law), with $\Delta K = \beta\,\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ and m ≈ 3–4 for metals. The log-log da/dN–ΔK curve is sigmoidal: below the threshold $\Delta K_{th}$ cracks don't grow; the straight middle is the Paris regime; growth runs away as $K_{max} \to K_{IC}$. Because ΔK rises with √a, growth compounds — most of the life elapses while the crack is small. Damage-tolerant design assumes the largest flaw inspection can miss, integrates Paris to the critical size, and sets the inspection interval at a fraction of that growth life.
topics.recall_prompt: Sketch the da/dN vs ΔK curve, write the Paris law, and walk through how damage-tolerant design turns crack growth into an inspection interval.
key_ideas: $da/dN = C\,\Delta K^m$ with $\Delta K = \beta\,\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$; m ≈ 3–4 for metals
Sigmoidal log-log curve: threshold $\Delta K_{th}$ (no growth) → straight Paris regime → fast fracture as $K_{max} \to K_{IC}$
ΔK grows with √a, so growth accelerates — most of the life is spent while the crack is small
Damage tolerance: assume the largest flaw NDT can MISS already exists; integrate to critical size $a_c$ from $K_{IC}$
Inspection interval = a fraction (commonly half) of the detectable-to-critical growth life, so every crack is seen before it's critical
Steep stress sensitivity: life scales ~$\Delta\sigma^{-m}$ — a 20% stress bump costs ~40% of growth life

cards.question: Sketch the fatigue crack growth curve — da/dN vs ΔK on log-log axes — with its three regions labeled.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Both axes logarithmic: ΔK horizontal, da/dN vertical
- A sigmoidal curve with three labeled regions
- Region I: steep plunge to a vertical asymptote at the threshold ΔK_th — below it, no measurable growth
- Region II: straight line (Paris regime), slope = m ≈ 3–4 for metals
- Region III: curve sweeping up toward a vertical asymptote where K_max → K_IC (fast fracture)
- Validity note: long cracks and small-scale yielding only — short cracks grow faster than the curve predicts

cards.question: Why does most of a cracked part's remaining life elapse while the crack is still small — and what does that imply about inspection?
cards.answer: $\Delta K = \beta\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ rises with crack length and $da/dN \propto \Delta K^m$, so growth compounds: the final doubling of crack size consumes only a few percent of the cycles. Implication: usable life is controlled by the smallest crack you can reliably FIND (NDT sensitivity), not by the critical size — better inspection buys far more life than tougher material.

cards.question: How does damage-tolerant design set the inspection interval for a wing skin?
cards.answer: Assume a crack equal to the largest flaw the NDT method can miss already exists at the worst location. Integrate the Paris law from that size to the critical size where $K_{max} = K_{IC}$ (or net-section failure). Divide that growth life by a safety factor — commonly 2 — so any real crack is inspected at least once, ideally twice, before it can go critical.

cards.question: Safe-life vs damage-tolerant design — what does each assume about flaws, and which do you pick for a helicopter rotor hub vs an airliner fuselage?
cards.answer: Safe-life assumes the part starts flaw-free and retires it at a scatter-factored fraction of demonstrated life — required where inspection is impractical and failure is instantly catastrophic (rotor hubs, landing gear). Damage tolerance assumes flaws exist from day one and guarantees inspections catch growth first — right for inspectable, slow-growing structure (fuselage and wing skin). Choosing wrong matters: damage tolerance on an uninspectable part is unconservative; safe-life on inspectable structure throws away serviceable hardware.

topics.title: Creep & stress relaxation
topics.subject: Mechanics of Materials
topics.content: Above roughly 0.3–0.4 of the absolute melting temperature, metals deform slowly under constant stress. The creep curve has three stages: primary (rate decelerates as strain hardening wins), secondary/steady-state (hardening and recovery balance → constant minimum rate, the design number), tertiary (necking and grain-boundary cavitation accelerate to rupture). Steady-state rate: $\dot{\epsilon} = A\,\sigma^n e^{-Q/RT}$ — dislocation climb at high stress (n ≈ 3–8), diffusional flow at low stress (n ≈ 1), the latter FASTER for fine grains. Larson-Miller $LM = T(C + \log t)$, C ≈ 20, T absolute, trades time against temperature so short hot tests predict long service. Stress relaxation is the same physics at constant strain: stress decays — hot bolted joints lose preload.
topics.recall_prompt: Sketch the three-stage creep curve, give the steady-state rate law and its mechanisms vs T/Tm, explain the Larson-Miller time–temperature trade, and contrast creep with stress relaxation.
key_ideas: Creep matters above ~0.3–0.4 $T/T_m$ (absolute — homologous temperature)
Three stages: primary (decelerating), secondary (constant minimum rate — the design basis), tertiary (accelerating to rupture via necking and cavitation)
$\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A\,\sigma^n e^{-Q/RT}$: dislocation climb n ≈ 3–8 at high stress; diffusional creep n ≈ 1 at low stress
Diffusional creep and boundary sliding speed up as grains shrink — Hall-Petch inverts hot; turbine blades go single-crystal
Larson-Miller $LM = T(C + \log t)$, C ≈ 20: equal LM ⇒ equal damage — test hot-and-short to predict cool-and-long
Stress relaxation = constant strain, decaying stress: bolted joints, press fits, plastic snap fits

cards.question: Sketch the strain–time creep curve for a metal under constant stress at high temperature, labeling all three stages.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Axes: strain (vertical) vs time (horizontal), at constant σ and T
- An instantaneous elastic (+ any plastic) strain jump at t = 0 — the curve does not start at zero strain
- Primary stage: slope decreasing (strain hardening winning)
- Secondary stage: straight segment — the constant minimum slope is the steady-state creep rate, the design quantity
- Tertiary stage: slope increasing, curve turning up to rupture (×)
- Trend note: raising σ or T steepens and shortens everything — secondary shrinks, rupture arrives sooner

cards.question: Creep vs stress relaxation — same physics, different boundary condition. Which one loosens a hot bolted joint, and why does the distinction matter?
cards.answer: Creep: constant stress, accumulating strain — a blade slowly lengthens. Relaxation: constant total strain, so elastic strain converts to creep strain and stress decays — that's the bolted joint: the clamped stretch is fixed, the bolt creeps, preload falls. Choosing wrong matters: a hot joint passes every static check and still leaks or rattles loose; the fix is re-torque schedules, creep-resistant fasteners, or spring elements that tolerate the lost stretch.

cards.question: Fine grains strengthen metals at room temperature (Hall-Petch). Why are creep-limited turbine blades grown as single crystals?
cards.answer: At high temperature the deformation paths run through the boundaries themselves: diffusional creep scales like $1/d^2$–$1/d^3$, and boundaries slide and cavitate (tertiary damage). More boundary area = faster creep and earlier rupture — Hall-Petch inverts. Directional solidification removes transverse boundaries; single-crystal blades remove them all.

cards.question: You must qualify a part for 100,000 hours at 550 °C. How does Larson-Miller let you skip the 11-year test, and when does the shortcut lie?
cards.answer: $LM = T(C + \log t)$, T absolute, C ≈ 20: equal parameter means equal creep damage, so test hotter for shorter at the same LM and read rupture strength off the material's master curve. It lies when the higher temperature changes the physics — new precipitate phases, oxidation, a mechanism shift from climb to diffusional flow — so extrapolate only within one mechanism regime.

topics.title: Stress concentrations & fits
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Geometric discontinuities multiply nominal stress: $\sigma_{max} = K_t \sigma_{nom}$, with $K_t$ from charts (Peterson) keyed to r/d and D/d. In fatigue use $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ — notch sensitivity q < 1 (small notches hurt less than $K_t$ says, especially in soft materials). Ductile static loading can ignore $K_t$ (local yielding redistributes); brittle and fatigue cannot. Fit classes (ISO H7/g6 etc.): clearance, transition, interference — interference fits add their own stresses (thick-cylinder eqs).
topics.recall_prompt: Explain Kt vs Kf and notch sensitivity, when stress concentrations can be ignored, and how fit classes (clearance/transition/interference) are specified.
key_ideas: $\sigma_{max} = K_t \sigma_{nom}$ — Kt from geometry charts (r/d, D/d)
Fatigue: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t-1)$, q = notch sensitivity (material + notch radius)
Static ductile: local yield redistributes → Kt often ignored; brittle/fatigue: never ignore
Generous fillets, relief grooves, spread load paths — design the concentration down
Fits: clearance (H7/g6), transition (H7/k6), interference (H7/p6) — hole-basis standard

cards.question: Why can you often ignore a stress concentration in a statically loaded ductile part but never in the same part under fatigue?
cards.answer: Static: the notch root yields locally, strain hardens, and load redistributes — net section carries it. Fatigue: cyclic stress at the root initiates a crack long before any beneficial redistribution; the concentration acts every cycle ($K_f$).

cards.question: A sharp shoulder has Kt = 3 but the measured fatigue knockdown is only ~2. Reconcile Kt vs Kf — and explain why upgrading to a stronger steel can make the notch WORSE.
cards.answer: Kt is pure elasticity and geometry; fatigue feels $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ with notch sensitivity q < 1. The elastic peak lives in a tiny, steeply graded volume: when the notch radius is small relative to the material's characteristic (Neuber/Peterson) length, the stress averaged over a grain-scale process zone sits well below the peak, so q drops. Hard, fine-grained, high-strength steels have tiny characteristic lengths → q → 1: the stronger steel feels nearly the full Kt, so notched fatigue life can fall even as $S_{ut}$ rises.

cards.question: What stresses does an interference fit add before any service load is applied, and where do press-fitted assemblies then fail in service?
cards.answer: The assembly squeeze creates an interface pressure (Lamé thick-cylinder equations): the hub goes into hoop tension, maximum at its bore — thin hubs can yield or split on assembly — while the shaft sees compression. Torque transmits by friction, ≈ $\mu p\,\pi d L\,(d/2)$. In rotating bending, the press edges add a stress concentration plus micro-slip → fretting fatigue at the hub faces is the classic failure; relieve it with raised seats, grooves, or generous edge radii.

topics.title: Bolted joints: preload, stiffness, failure
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Torque sets preload: $T = K d F_i$ (K ≈ 0.2 dry). Joint as springs: bolt stiffness $k_b \sim EA/L$ in series with member stiffness $k_m$ (frustum cones — members are usually stiffer). External load P splits: bolt sees only $C\,P$ where $C = k_b/(k_b+k_m)$ is small — preload shields the bolt from fatigue. Failure checks: bolt tension/fatigue, thread stripping, member bearing, pull-through, joint separation ($P > F_i/(1-C)$). Preload also provides friction for shear joints.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk through a bolted-joint analysis: torque-preload, the stiffness split (why the bolt sees little of the external load), separation, and the list of failure modes to check.
key_ideas: $T = KdF_i$, K ≈ 0.2 — torque is a crude preload control (±25%)
$k_b \approx EA/L$; member stiffness from frustum cones, usually $k_m \gg k_b$
Bolt load increment = $C P$, $C = k_b/(k_b+k_m)$ → preload shields the bolt from fatigue
Separation when external load exceeds $F_i/(1-C)$ — then the bolt takes everything
Check: bolt yield/fatigue, thread strip, bearing, pull-through, slip (friction joints)

cards.question: Why does increasing preload usually IMPROVE bolt fatigue life, even though it raises mean stress?
cards.answer: The bolt only sees the fraction $C = k_b/(k_b+k_m)$ of the fluctuating external load while the joint stays clamped — amplitude is tiny. Higher preload keeps the joint from separating (where the bolt would take the full alternating load). Fatigue is amplitude-driven; the mean-stress penalty is the smaller effect.

cards.question: Why are long, thin bolts better than short, fat ones for fatigue-loaded joints?
cards.answer: They're springier ($k_b = EA/L$ drops), so $C$ shrinks and the bolt sees even less of the alternating load — and they hold preload better against embedding/relaxation.

cards.question: Two identical joints torqued to the same spec — one assembled dry, one with anti-seize. Which is in danger, and of what?
cards.answer: The lubricated one: $T = KdF_i$, and lube cuts the nut factor from ≈0.2 toward ≈0.1–0.15, so the same torque delivers up to ~50–100% more preload — enough to yield the bolt or crush the joint. Roughly 90% of installation torque fights friction (~50% under-head, ~40% threads), so friction scatter IS preload scatter. Critical joints control lubrication explicitly or measure stretch/turn-of-nut instead of torque.

topics.title: Hertzian contact stress
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Curved bodies touch over a tiny patch, so modest loads create GPa-scale pressures. Hertz: sphere contact gives a circular patch with $p_{max} = 3F/(2\pi a^2)$ (1.5× the mean); cylinders give a strip with $p_{max} = 2F/(\pi b L)$. The patch grows with load, so contact stiffens nonlinearly ($F \propto \delta^{3/2}$ for spheres). Under the patch center the stress state is near-hydrostatic compression — first yield needs $p_{max} \approx 1.6\,\sigma_y$ — and the maximum shear hides BELOW the surface (≈0.3 $p_{max}$ at depth ≈0.48a for spheres, ≈0.8b for cylinders). That's why rolling-contact fatigue (gear pitting, bearing spalling) starts subsurface. Hertz assumes frictionless, elastic, non-conforming contact: sliding traction drags the shear peak to the surface; conforming or plastic contacts need other tools.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk through Hertzian contact: pressure distribution and peak for sphere and cylinder, why first yield and pitting start below the surface, the nonlinear stiffness, and the assumptions whose violation kills the theory.
key_ideas: Sphere: circular patch, $p_{max} = 3F/(2\pi a^2)$ = 1.5× mean; cylinder: strip, $p_{max} = 2F/(\pi b L)$ — semi-elliptical pressure distributions
Effective properties combine the pair: $1/E^* = (1-\nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2)/E_2$, $1/R = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2$
Patch grows with load → nonlinear stiffening: $F \propto \delta^{3/2}$ for point contact
Contact center is near-hydrostatic compression → first yield only at $p_{max} \approx 1.6\,\sigma_y$ (same constraint physics as hardness ≈ 3× flow stress)
Max shear ≈ 0.3 $p_{max}$ at depth ≈ 0.48a (sphere) / 0.8b (cylinder) → rolling-contact fatigue initiates subsurface
Breaks down with sliding friction (shear moves to the surface), conforming geometry, plasticity, or an EHL film reshaping the pressure

cards.question: Why do gear pitting and bearing spalling initiate below the surface rather than at it?
cards.answer: In pure rolling Hertz contact the maximum shear (≈0.3 $p_{max}$) sits at a depth of roughly half the contact half-width, while the surface itself is in near-hydrostatic compression that suppresses yielding. Fatigue cracks nucleate at that subsurface shear peak — often at an inclusion — and grow up to flake out a spall. Add sliding traction and the shear peak migrates to the surface: surface distress and micropitting instead.

cards.question: A bearing raceway runs at 3 GPa contact pressure — several times the steel's yield strength. Why doesn't it simply squash?
cards.answer: Under the contact all three principal stresses are large, compressive, and nearly equal — near-hydrostatic. Yield is driven by principal-stress differences (von Mises), which stay modest, so first yield doesn't occur until $p_{max} \approx 1.6\,\sigma_y$ and full plasticity near 3$\sigma_y$ — the same ~3× constraint factor that makes hardness ≈ 3× flow stress.

cards.question: Why is contact stiffness nonlinear — and which Hertz assumptions, when violated, invalidate the whole solution?
cards.answer: The patch grows with load, recruiting area: $F \propto \delta^{3/2}$ for a sphere, so doubling the load raises approach only ~1.6×. The solution assumes elastic, frictionless, non-conforming contact with patch ≪ body radii. Sliding friction adds surface shear (moves the critical point up), conforming contacts (ball in a close race, gear past the pitch point) violate the small-patch assumption, and plasticity or an EHL pressure spike rewrites the distribution.

topics.title: Gears: AGMA bending & contact stress
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Two failure modes, two equations. Tooth-root bending (Lewis basis): $\sigma_b = \frac{W_t p_d}{F J}\cdot\frac{K_a K_m K_s K_b K_I}{K_v}$ — fatigue at the root fillet, sized by geometry factor J. Surface contact (pitting, Hertzian): $\sigma_c = C_p\sqrt{\frac{W_t C_a C_m C_s C_f}{F\, I\, d\, C_v}}$ — compare to surface strength. K/C factors derate for application shock, mounting/misalignment, size, dynamics. Bending breaks teeth; pitting wears flanks — pitting usually governs long-life drives.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain the two AGMA gear checks — root bending vs surface contact — what each K/C factor accounts for, and which failure mode governs when.
key_ideas: Bending: $\sigma_b = W_t p_d/(FJ) \times$ K-factors — root fillet fatigue, J = geometry
Contact: $\sigma_c = C_p\sqrt{W_t \ldots/(F I d)}$ — Hertzian pitting on the flank
Factors derate for application ($K_a$), misalignment ($K_m$), dynamics ($K_v$), size, rim
Tooth breakage = bending; pitting/spalling = contact — pitting governs long-life drives
$W_t$ = tangential force from torque; face width F and diametral pitch trade size vs stress

cards.question: A gearbox must last 10^9 cycles. Which AGMA check almost certainly governs, and why?
cards.answer: Surface contact (pitting): contact-strength S-N keeps falling with cycles (no real endurance limit) while bending often has one — so at very long life the flank pits before a root cracks.

cards.question: Pitting and root bending are both fatigue — why do designers carry a bigger safety margin against the bending mode even when pitting governs the sizing?
cards.answer: A root crack snaps the whole tooth: instant loss of drive, debris through the mesh, often cascading failure — with little warning. Pitting is progressive and announces itself in noise, vibration, and oil debris long before the gear stops, allowing condition-based replacement. Same math, very different consequence severity, so bending gets the larger reliability factor.

cards.question: Doubling face width F halves nominal tooth stress — why does AGMA's Km claw part of that back, and when does widening stop paying?
cards.answer: Km (load distribution) accounts for non-uniform contact across the face from shaft slope, housing tolerance, and lead error: the wider the face, the harder it is to keep load centered, so Km grows with F and with flimsy mounting. Past roughly 3–5× the circular pitch, the extra width carries load only at one end — you've bought an edge stress concentration, not capacity — unless you stiffen the shafts or crown the teeth.

topics.title: Rolling bearings: L10 life
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Bearing life is statistical: $L_{10}$ = revolutions 90% of identical bearings survive. $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ (millions of revs), p = 3 for balls, 10/3 for rollers; C = dynamic rating, P = equivalent load $P = XF_r + YF_a$. Life is wildly load-sensitive (ball: halving load gives 8× life). Refinements: $a_1$ reliability factor, lubrication/contamination factors (ISO modified life). Static check against C0 for brinelling when stationary/shock loaded.
topics.recall_prompt: Define L10 and the life equation — exponents, equivalent load, what the refinement factors add, and why bearing life is so sensitive to load.
key_ideas: $L_{10}$: 90%-survival life — bearings are inherently statistical (subsurface fatigue)
$L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ million revs; p = 3 balls, 10/3 rollers
Equivalent load $P = XF_r + YF_a$ combines radial + axial
Halving load → ~8× ball-bearing life; overload is catastrophic to life
Modified life adds reliability, lubrication, contamination factors; check C0 static (brinelling)

cards.question: Marketing wants 99% bearing reliability instead of 90%. Roughly what happens to rated life and why?
cards.answer: Life drops to roughly a fifth ($a_1 \approx 0.21$): the Weibull failure distribution is broad, so demanding the 1st percentile instead of the 10th costs most of the rated revolutions. Reliability is expensive in fatigue-governed components.

cards.question: A machine sits parked for months under vibrating load. Why is the L10 equation the wrong tool for its bearings, and what's the right check?
cards.answer: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ counts revolutions — it assumes rotation sweeps the contact stress around the whole raceway. Stationary, the same balls hammer the same spots: true brinelling (dents from shock exceeding the static capacity) or false brinelling (fretting wear under vibration with no lubricant replenishment). Check P against the static rating C0 with a safety factor, and protect idle or transported machines — lock the rotor or rotate it periodically.

cards.question: Why do lubricant film and cleanliness swing bearing life so strongly that ISO gives them their own life factor?
cards.answer: Rated life assumes failure starts subsurface. With a full EHL film (thickness ≳ ~3× the composite surface roughness) the surfaces never touch and life can exceed catalog severalfold; a starved film or a hard particle dents the raceway and converts failure to surface-initiated fatigue — every dent is a stress raiser that spalls early. Hence the ISO $a_{ISO}$ factor spans orders of magnitude: dirty, starved contacts can lose 10× life.

topics.title: Shaft design under combined loading
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: A transmission shaft carries steady torque plus transverse gear/pulley loads — and because it rotates, every surface fiber sees fully reversed bending each revolution even when the loads are fixed in space. Standard procedure (Shigley DE-Goodman): alternating von Mises stress from bending, $\sigma_a' = 32K_f M_a/(\pi d^3)$; mean from steady torsion, $\sigma_m' = \sqrt{3}\cdot 16K_{fs}T_m/(\pi d^3)$; combine on Goodman with the Marin-corrected $S_e$ and solve for d. The critical section sits at a stress concentration — bearing shoulder, keyseat ($K_f \approx 2$ in bending), groove — not at the nominal max-moment point. Strength often isn't binding: slope at rolling bearings, deflection at gear meshes, and the first critical speed frequently demand a fatter shaft than fatigue does.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk through sizing a rotating shaft under steady torque + bending: which stress goes in the alternating bin vs the mean bin, where Kf applies, the DE-Goodman combination, and the stiffness checks that often govern instead.
key_ideas: Rotation turns a fixed transverse load into fully reversed bending (R = −1) at every fiber; steady torque stays a mean shear
DE-Goodman: $\sigma_a' = 32K_f M_a/(\pi d^3)$, $\sigma_m' = \sqrt{3}\cdot 16K_{fs}T_m/(\pi d^3)$, then $\sigma_a'/S_e + \sigma_m'/S_{ut} = 1/n$
Critical location = shoulder, keyseat ($K_f \approx 2$ bending), or groove — wherever $K_f M/d^3$ peaks, not where M peaks
Stress falls with $d^3$ — small diameter changes buy large margins ($d \propto n^{1/3}$)
Stiffness often governs: slope limits at rolling bearings and deflection limits at gear meshes; long shafts are sized by deflection
Keep the first critical speed well clear of operating speed (commonly ≥ 2× margin)

cards.question: A shaft sees a constant-direction transverse load and a steady torque — nothing fluctuates. Why is sizing it still a fatigue problem?
cards.answer: Because the shaft rotates through the fixed bending field: each surface fiber cycles tension → compression once per revolution — fully reversed bending at shaft speed, so 10⁸ cycles arrives in weeks. The steady torque contributes only mean shear. The canonical load case is therefore alternating bending + mean torsion → DE-Goodman against the Marin-corrected $S_e$.

cards.question: Why do keyseats and bearing shoulders, not the nominal diameter, decide where a shaft fails — and what are the cheap fixes?
cards.answer: Each feature carries $K_f$ ≈ 1.5–2+ (a profiled keyseat is ≈2 in bending), halving local fatigue strength, so the critical point is where $K_f M/d^3$ peaks — often a shoulder next to a bearing, not the max-moment plane. Fixes are geometric: bigger fillet radii, relief grooves, sled-runner instead of end-milled keyseats, or move the shoulder out of the high-moment zone. Material upgrades barely help — notch sensitivity rises with strength.

cards.question: A shaft passes DE-Goodman with n = 2. Name the checks that can still reject it — and why a stronger alloy fixes none of them.
cards.answer: Slope at rolling bearings (misaligned races collapse bearing life), relative deflection at gear meshes (load concentrates at tooth ends, Km climbs), and the first critical speed (keep ~2× margin or sprint through it). All scale with $EI$ and span — stiffness, not strength — and every steel shares E ≈ 200 GPa, so only diameter, span, and bearing placement move them.

topics.title: Helical compression springs
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: A compression spring is a torsion bar wound into a helix: the axial force F acts at moment arm D/2 and twists the wire, $\tau = K_w\,8FD/(\pi d^3)$, where the Wahl factor $K_w = (4C-1)/(4C-4) + 0.615/C$ adds the curvature concentration (shear piles up on the coil's inside) plus direct shear; spring index C = D/d is kept ~6–12. Rate: $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N_a)$ — wire diameter to the fourth power, coil diameter cubed and active coils in the denominator; end treatment (squared, ground) sets $N_a$. Checks: stress at solid height, shear fatigue of the wire (shot peening and presetting leave helpful residual stress), lateral buckling of slender springs (free length ≳ ~5× D needs a guide), and surge — the spring's own longitudinal resonance — in high-speed cyclic service.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain where the stress in a helical compression spring lives, write the rate and Wahl-corrected stress equations, and walk the failure checks: solid-height stress, fatigue, buckling, surge.
key_ideas: The wire is in torsion: $\tau = K_w\,8FD/(\pi d^3)$ — axial force × moment arm D/2 twists the wire
Wahl $K_w = (4C-1)/(4C-4) + 0.615/C$: curvature concentrates shear on the coil ID; worse at low index C = D/d (keep ~6–12)
Rate $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N_a)$ — d⁴ sensitivity; squared/ground ends reduce active coils $N_a$
Check stress at solid height — assembly or bottoming must not yield the spring
Slender springs buckle laterally (free length ≳ ~5× D, end-condition dependent) → guide over a rod or in a bore
Surge = longitudinal resonance of the coil line; keep the first surge frequency well above the forcing frequency (rules of thumb ≥ ~13×)
Presetting and shot peening leave favorable residual stresses → higher static capacity and fatigue life

cards.question: An axially loaded compression spring fails in shear, starting at the inside of the coil. Why there?
cards.answer: The axial force acts at moment arm D/2 about the wire axis — the spring is a coiled torsion bar, so wire stress is $\tau = K_w\,8FD/(\pi d^3)$. Coil curvature makes the inner fiber shorter, concentrating the twist there, and the transverse direct shear adds on the same side: the Wahl factor (≈1.2 at C = 8, worse for tighter coils) captures both. Fatigue cracks therefore start at the coil ID.

cards.question: You need double the spring rate in the same envelope. Compare the three levers — wire diameter, coil diameter, active coils — and what each one costs.
cards.answer: $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N_a)$. Wire diameter is strongest: +19% on d doubles k (d⁴), but solid height and weight grow. Halving $N_a$ doubles k, but the same working deflection now needs double force → double wire stress, and fewer coils means less stress relief at solid. Shrinking D by 21% doubles k (D³) and lowers stress per newton (τ ∝ FD), but the index C falls, driving the Wahl factor up and making winding harder. There's no free doubling — recheck solid-height stress whichever lever you pull.

cards.question: What is spring surge, and why can a valve spring that passes every static and fatigue check still fail at high engine speed?
cards.answer: The spring is a distributed mass-elastic line with its own longitudinal natural frequencies. When cam harmonics approach a surge mode, compression waves travel along the coil: individual coils over-travel, momentarily clash or unload, wire stress spikes far beyond the quasi-static prediction, and valve control is lost. Design rule: put the first surge frequency well above the drive frequency (classic rules ≥ ~13×) so the strong low cam harmonics can't find it.

topics.title: Thermal stress & strain
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: Free expansion: $\epsilon = \alpha \Delta T$, no stress. Stress appears only when expansion is constrained: full constraint → $\sigma = -E\alpha\Delta T$ (compressive when heated). Partial constraint, dissimilar materials (bimetallic bending, CTE mismatch in joints/PCBs/composites), and thermal gradients (quench cracking) are the standard scenarios. Superpose: compute free expansion, apply the force needed to satisfy compatibility.
topics.recall_prompt: When does ΔT produce stress and when only strain? Work the standard scenarios: full constraint, two-bar/dissimilar materials, and gradients.
key_ideas: Unconstrained ΔT → strain $\alpha\Delta T$, zero stress
Fully constrained: $\sigma = -E\alpha\Delta T$ — heated bar goes into compression
Dissimilar materials sharing a boundary: CTE mismatch → internal stress, bending (bimetal)
Gradients self-constrain: surface vs core (quench cracks, thermal shock)
Method: free expansion + compatibility force superposition

cards.question: Why does a rapid quench crack thick ceramic or glass parts but not thin ones?
cards.answer: The surface cools and contracts while the core is still hot — the core constrains the surface, putting it in tension. Thicker sections sustain larger ΔT between surface and core (Biot number grows), so the self-constraint stress exceeds the (flaw-limited) tensile strength.

cards.question: A steel bolt clamps an aluminum housing and the assembly heats uniformly by 80 °C — no gradients anywhere. Why does the joint get tighter, and what must you check?
cards.answer: CTE mismatch: aluminum (α ≈ 23 µm/m·°C) wants to grow about twice as much as the steel bolt (α ≈ 12) allows, so compatibility forces extra bolt stretch — preload climbs with temperature. Check bolt yield hot and gasket/member crush; then check the cold case, because cooling LOOSENS the same joint (preload loss → leaks, slip, fatigue). The load is the differential expansion, not the temperature itself.

cards.question: Why doesn't a fully constrained bar's thermal stress depend on its length — and what DOES scale with length, biting pipelines and rails?
cards.answer: $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$ comes from strain compatibility, so a 1 m and a 100 m bar see identical stress (steel at ΔT = 50 °C → ~120 MPa either way). What grows with length is the free displacement $\alpha\Delta T\,L$ — millimeters per 10 m — which must be absorbed somewhere. That's why pipelines get expansion loops and bridges get sliding bearings: park the displacement deliberately, or it shows up as buckling (rail sun kink) or enormous anchor loads.

topics.title: FEA fundamentals: stiffness method
topics.subject: Machine Element Design
topics.content: FEM solves $[K]\{d\} = \{r\}$: assemble element stiffness matrices (from shape functions interpolating displacement, $k = \int B^T E B\, dV$), apply boundary conditions, solve for nodal displacements, recover strains/stresses by differentiation. 3-D Hooke's law: $\epsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{E}[(1+\nu)\sigma_{ij} - \nu\sigma_{kk}\delta_{ij}]$. Strain energy density $u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\epsilon$ underlies the variational formulation. Displacements are the primary unknowns — stress is a derived (less accurate) quantity, which is why stress needs finer mesh than deflection.
topics.recall_prompt: Walk the FEM pipeline: shape functions → element stiffness → assembly → [K]{d}={r} → stress recovery, and explain why stress converges slower than displacement.
key_ideas: Shape functions interpolate displacement between nodes; order sets element accuracy
$[K]\{d\} = \{r\}$: assemble, constrain (kill rigid-body modes), solve
Stress is recovered by differentiating displacement → one order less accurate
Generalized Hooke: $\epsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{E}[(1+\nu)\sigma_{ij} - \nu\sigma_{kk}\delta_{ij}]$
Energy view: equilibrium = minimum potential energy; $u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\epsilon$
2D idealizations: plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$, thin parts) vs plane strain ($\epsilon_z = 0$, long/constrained parts)

cards.question: Why does a displacement result converge with a coarse mesh while the stress at the same spot is still wrong?
cards.answer: Displacement is the primary solved field; stress comes from its derivative, losing one order of accuracy — and peak stress lives in gradients the coarse shape functions can't represent. Refine (or use higher-order elements) where stress matters; check convergence on the stress, not the deflection.

cards.question: Your FEA solver reports a singular stiffness matrix. What are the usual suspects?
cards.answer: Unconstrained rigid-body modes (part can translate/rotate freely — missing BCs), disconnected mesh regions, or mechanisms (e.g., pin-jointed chain). The matrix has zero-energy modes, so it can't be inverted.

cards.question: Plane stress vs plane strain — which 2D idealization for a thin bracket vs the mid-section of a long dam, and what goes wrong if you swap them?
cards.answer: Plane stress sets $\sigma_z = 0$ — right for thin parts whose faces are free to contract (sheet, brackets). Plane strain sets $\epsilon_z = 0$ — right where material is constrained along its own length (dams, pipe cross-sections, the mid-thickness of thick parts); it develops $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)$ and behaves stiffer. Swapping shifts stiffness by ν² effects, but the worse error is triaxiality: plane stress hides the constraint that suppresses yielding and drops fracture toughness in thick sections.
